steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: The sentence: "The part of a compound that determines the compound's color is ______ the chromophore." The verb "called" takes "the chromophore" as its required object complement — the name being assigned. Rule: no punctuation separates a verb from its required complement.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A: called—: ".color is called— the chromophore." An em dash after "called" creates an abrupt break before the required complement, making the verb feel dangling. ✗
  • Choice B — called;: ".color is called; the chromophore." A semicolon joins two independent clauses, but "the chromophore" alone is not an independent clause — it is the object complement of "called." ✗
  • Choice C — called,: ".color is called, the chromophore." A comma between a verb and its required object complement is never correct; it wrongly separates "called" from what it names. ✗
  • Choice D — called: ".color is called the chromophore." No punctuation — correct because "the chromophore" is the required object complement of "called." ✓
That matches Choice D — called.
